audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a telephone conversation between an official at a village hall and a woman who wants to hire a public room.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Oh hello, I wanted to inquire about hiring a room in the village hall for the evening of September the first.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Let me just see.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Uh. Yes, we have both rooms available that evening.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: There's our Main Hall, that's got seating for 200 people.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Or there's the Charlton Room.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: The Main Hall seat's 200, so 200 has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully, and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Oh hello, I wanted to inquire about hiring a room in the village hall for the evening of September the first.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Let me just see.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Uh. Yes, we have both rooms available that evening.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: There's our Main Hall, that's got seating for 200 people.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Or there's the Charlton Room.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Sorry?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The Charlton Room, CHARLTON.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: That's got seating for up to 100.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Well, we're organizing a dinner to raise money for a charity, and we're hoping for at least 150 people.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So, I think we'll go for the Main Hall.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: How much would that cost?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Let's see, um, you wanted it for the evening of September the first.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Yes, that's a Saturday.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So from 6 pm to midnight, that would be £115 that's the weekend price.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: It's £75 on weekdays.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: That's all right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And I have to tell you there's also a deposit of £250 which is returnable of course, as long as there's no damage.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: But we do insist that this is paid in cash.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: We don't take cards for that.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: You can pay the actual rent of the room, however you like though.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Cash, credit card, cheque...
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Oh, well, I suppose that's OK.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So does the charge include use of tables and chairs and so on?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: And what about parking?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, that's all included.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: The only thing that isn't included is... you said you were organizing a dinner.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, you'll have to pay extra for the kitchen if you want to use that.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: It's got very good facilities, good quality cookers and fridges and so on.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: OK, well I suppose that's all right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We can cover the cost in our entry charges.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right, so I'll make a note of that.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Now, there are just one or two things you need to think about before the event.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: For example, you'll have to see about getting a license if you're planning to have any music during the meal.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Oh, really?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's quite straightforward.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: I'll give you the details later on.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: And about a week or 10 days before your event, you'll need to contact the caretaker, that's Mr Evans.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Uh. To make the arrangements for entry, he'll sort that out with you.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: And do I give him the payment as well?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No, you do that directly with me.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Right, now is there anything I need to know about what happens during the event?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, as you'll be aware, of course, the building is no smoking throughout.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Of course.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Now are you having a band?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, they'll have a lot of equipment, so rather than using the front door, they should park their van round the back, and use the stage door there.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: You can open that from inside, but don't forget to lock it at the end.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And talking of bands, I'm sure I don't need to tell you this, but you must make sure that no one fiddles about with the black box by the fire door.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: That's a system that cuts in when the volume reaches a certain level, it's a legal requirement.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Anyway, we want people to be able to talk to one another.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So we don't want anything too loud.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, that reminds me, we'll be having speeches.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Are there any microphones available?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Just let the caretaker know, he'll get those for you.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Right, now when the event is over, we do ask that the premises are left in good condition.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: So there's a locked cupboard, and you'll be informed of the code you need to open that.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: It's got all the cleaning equipment, brushes and detergent and so on.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Right, so what do we need to do after everyone's gone?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Sweep the floors, I suppose.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, actually they have to be washed, not just swept.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Then you'll be provided with black plastic bags, so all the rubbish must be collected up and left outside the door.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Of course, we'll make sure everything's left tidy.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, and I forgot to ask.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I presume we can have decorations in the room.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, but you must take them down afterwards.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And the chairs and tables should be stacked up neatly at the back of the room.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: I'll make sure I've got a few people to help me.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a guide talking to a group of visitors to a farm.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully, and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Welcome to the Fiddy Working Heritage Farm.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This open air museum gives you the experience of agriculture and rural life in the English countryside.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At the end of the 19th century.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So you'll see a typical farm of that period, and like me, all the staff are dressed in clothes of that time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I must give you some advice and safety tips before we go any further.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As it's a working farm, please don't frighten or injure the animals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We have a lot here, and many of them are breeds that are now quite rare.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And do stay at a safe distance from the tools.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some of them have sharp points, which can be pretty dangerous, so please don't touch them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We don't want any accidents, do we?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The ground is very uneven, and you might slip if you're wearing sandals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So I'm glad to see you're all wearing shoes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We always advise people to do that.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, children of all ages are very welcome here, and usually even very young children love the ducks and lambs, so do bring them along next time you come.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I don't think any of you have brought dogs with you, but in case you have, I'm afraid they'll have to stay in the car park, unless they're guide dogs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'm sure you'll understand that they could cause a lot of problems on a farm.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now let me give you some idea of the layout of the farm.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The building where you bought your tickets is the New Barn, immediately to your right, and we're now at the beginning of the main path to the farmland.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And of course, the car park is on your left.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The scarecrow you can see in the car park in the corner beside the main path is a traditional figure for keeping the birds away from crops.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But our scarecrow is a permanent sculpture.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's taller than a human being, so you can see it from quite a distance.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you look ahead of you, you'll see a maze.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's opposite the New Barn, beside the side path that branches off to the right, just over there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The maze is made out of hedges, which are too tall for young children to see over them, but it's quite small, so you can't get lost in it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, can you see the bridge crossing the fish pool further up the main path?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you want to go to the cafe, go towards the bridge and turn right just before it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Walk along the side path, and the café's on the first bend you come to.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The building was originally the schoolhouse, and it's well over 100 years old.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As you may know, we run skills workshops here, where you can learn traditional crafts like woodwork and basket making.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You can see examples of the work.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And talk to someone about the courses in the Black Barn.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you take the side path to the right here, just by the New Barn, you'll come to the Black Barn, just where the path first bends.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now I mustn't forget to tell you about picnicking, as I can see some of you have brought your lunch with you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You can picnic in the field.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Though do clear up behind you, of course.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or if you'd prefer a covered picnic area, there's one near the farmyard, just after you cross the bridge.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's a covered picnic spot on the right.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the last thing to mention is Fiddy House itself.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: From here you can cross the bridge, then walk along the footpath through the field to the left of the farmyard.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That goes to the house, and it'll give you a lovely view of it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's certainly worth a few photographs, but as it's a private home, I'm afraid you can't go inside.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Right. Well, if you're all ready, we'll set off on our tour of the farm.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: At the end of the 19th century.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So you'll see a typical farm of that period, and like me, all the staff are dressed in clothes of that time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I must give you some advice and safety tips before we go any further.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As it's a working farm, please don't frighten or injure the animals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We have a lot here, and many of them are breeds that are now quite rare.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And do stay at a safe distance from the tools.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some of them have sharp points, which can be pretty dangerous, so please don't touch them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We don't want any accidents, do we?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The ground is very uneven, and you might slip if you're wearing sandals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So I'm glad to see you're all wearing shoes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We always advise people to do that.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, children of all ages are very welcome here, and usually even very young children love the ducks and lambs, so do bring them along next time you come.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I don't think any of you have brought dogs with you, but in case you have, I'm afraid they'll have to stay in the car park, unless they're guide dogs.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I'm sure you'll understand that they could cause a lot of problems on a farm.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now let me give you some idea of the layout of the farm.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The building where you bought your tickets is the New Barn, immediately to your right, and we're now at the beginning of the main path to the farmland.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And of course, the car park is on your left.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The scarecrow you can see in the car park in the corner beside the main path is a traditional figure for keeping the birds away from crops.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But our scarecrow is a permanent sculpture.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's taller than a human being, so you can see it from quite a distance.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you look ahead of you, you'll see a maze.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's opposite the New Barn, beside the side path that branches off to the right, just over there.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The maze is made out of hedges, which are too tall for young children to see over them, but it's quite small, so you can't get lost in it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, can you see the bridge crossing the fish pool further up the main path?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you want to go to the cafe, go towards the bridge and turn right just before it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Walk along the side path, and the café's on the first bend you come to.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The building was originally the schoolhouse, and it's well over 100 years old.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As you may know, we run skills workshops here, where you can learn traditional crafts like woodwork and basket making.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You can see examples of the work.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And talk to someone about the courses in the Black Barn.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you take the side path to the right here, just by the New Barn, you'll come to the Black Barn, just where the path first bends.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now I mustn't forget to tell you about picnicking, as I can see some of you have brought your lunch with you.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You can picnic in the field.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Though do clear up behind you, of course.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Or if you'd prefer a covered picnic area, there's one near the farmyard, just after you cross the bridge.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There's a covered picnic spot on the right.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And the last thing to mention is Fiddy House itself.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: From here you can cross the bridge, then walk along the footpath through the field to the left of the farmyard.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That goes to the house, and it'll give you a lovely view of it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's certainly worth a few photographs, but as it's a private home, I'm afraid you can't go inside.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Right. Well, if you're all ready, we'll set off on our tour of the farm.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: take care not to harm any <input>
not bring <input> into the farm, with certain exceptions
items.title: Visitors should

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two psychology students called Lisa and Greg discussing a project they have to do.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 27.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 21 to 27.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, Greg.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: So I finally managed to read the article you mentioned, the one about the study on gender in physics.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: About the study of college students done by Akira Miyake and his team?
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Yeah, I was interested that the researchers were actually a mix of psychologists and physicists.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: That's an unusual combination.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: I got a little confused at first about which students the study was based on.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: They weren't actually majoring in physics, they were majoring in what's known as the STEM disciplines.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: That's science, technology, engineering, and...
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: ...and math.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Yes, but they were all doing physics courses as part of their studies.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: That's correct.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: So, as I understood it, Miyake and co started from the fact that women are underrepresented in introductory physics courses at college.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: And also that on average, the women who do enroll on these courses perform more poorly than the men.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: No one really knows why this is the case.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Yeah, but what the researchers wanted to find out was basically what they could do about the relatively low level of the women's results, but in order to find a solution.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: They needed to find out more about the nature of the problem.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Now let's see if I can remember, it was that in the physics class, the female students thought the male students all assumed that women weren't any good at physics.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Was that it?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: And they thought that the men expected them to get poor results in their tests.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: That's what the women thought, and that made them nervous, so they did get poor results.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: But actually they were wrong.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: No one was making any assumptions about the female students at all.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Anyway, what Miyake's team did was quite simple.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Getting the students to do some writing before they went into the physics class.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: What did they call it?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Values-affirmation.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: They had to write an essay focusing on things that were significant to them.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Not particularly to do with the subject they were studying, but more general things like music or people who mattered to them.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: So the idea of doing the writing is that this gets the students thinking in a positive way.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: And putting these thoughts into words can relax them and help them overcome the psychological factors that lead to poor performance.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: But what the researchers in the study hadn't expected was that this one activity raised the women's physics grades from the C to the B range.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: A huge change.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Pity it wasn't to an A, but still!
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: No, but it does suggest that the women were seriously underperforming beforehand in comparison with the men.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Mind you, Miyake's article left out a lot of details, like did the students do the writing just once or several times, and had they been told why they were doing the writing?
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: That might have affected the results.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: You mean if they know the researchers thought it might help them to improve, then they'd just try to fulfill that expectation?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 28 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 28 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So anyway, I thought for our project we could do a similar study, but investigate whether it really was the writing activity that had that result.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: OK, so we could ask them to do a writing task about something completely different, something more factual, like a general knowledge topic.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Maybe, or we could have half the students doing a writing task, and half doing something else like an oral task.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Or even, half do the same writing task as in the original research, and half do a factual writing task.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Then we'd see if it really is the topic that made the difference, or something else.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: That's it.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: So, at our meeting with the supervisor on Monday, we can tell him we've decided on our project.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: We should have our aims ready by then.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: I suppose we need to read the original study.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: The article is just a summary.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: And there was another article I read by Smolinsky.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: It was about her research on how women and men perform in mixed teams in class,
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: compared with single-sex teams and on their own.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Let me guess... the women were better at teamwork.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: That's what I expected, but actually the men and the women got the same results.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Whether they were working in teams or on their own.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Hmm. But I guess it's not that relevant to us.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: What worries me anyway is how we're going to get everything done in the time.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: We'll be OK, now we know what we're doing.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Though, I'm not clear how we assess whether the students in our experiment actually make any progress or not.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: No, we may need some advice on that.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: The main thing's to make sure we have the right size sample, uh, not too big or too small.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: That shouldn't be difficult.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Right, what do we need to do next?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: We could have a look at the timetable for the science classes, or perhaps we should just make an appointment to see one of the science professors.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: That'd be better.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: And we could even get to observe one of the classes.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: What for?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Well, OK, maybe let's just go with your idea.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Right, well, I think that's everything for now.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: The students in Akira Miyake's study were all majoring in
options: physics.
psychology or physics.
science, technology, engineering or mathematics.

items.title: The aim of Miyake's study was to investigate
options: what kind of women choose to study physics.
a way of improving women's performance in physics.
whether fewer women than men study physics at college.

items.title: The female physics students were wrong to believe that
options: the teachers marked them in an unfair way.
the male students expected them to do badly.
their test results were lower than the male students'.

items.title: Miyake's team asked the students to write about
options: what they enjoyed about studying physics.
the successful experiences of other people.
something that was important to them personally.

items.title: What was the aim of the writing exercise done by the subjects?
options: to reduce stress
to strengthen verbal ability
to encourage logical thinking

items.title: What surprised the researchers about the study?
options: how few students managed to get A grades
the positive impact it had on physics results for women
the difference between male and female performance

items.title: Greg and Lisa think Miyake's results could have been affected by
options: the length of the writing task.
the number of students who took part.
the information the students were given.

items.title: Greg and Lisa decide that in their own project, they will compare the effects of
options: two different writing tasks.
a writing task with an oral task.
two different oral tasks.

items.title: The main finding of Smolinsky's research was that class teamwork activities
options: were most effective when done by all-women groups.
had no effect on the performance of men or women.
improved the results of men more than of women.

items.title: What will Lisa and Greg do next?
options: talk to a professor
observe a science class
look at the science timetable

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a student presentation about the variety of different species that live in the world's oceans.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I've been looking at ocean biodiversity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's the diversity of species that live in the world's oceans.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: About 20 years ago, biologists developed the idea of what they called biodiversity hotspots.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These are the areas which have the greatest mixture of species, so one example is Madagascar.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These hotspots are significant.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because they allow us to locate key areas for focusing efforts at conservation.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Biologists can identify hotspots on land fairly easily.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But until recently, very little was known about species distribution and diversity in the oceans, and no one even knew if hotspots existed there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then a Canadian biologist called Boris Worm did some research in 2005 on data on ocean species that he got from the fishing industry.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Worm located five hotspots for large ocean predators like sharks, and looked at what they had in common.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The main thing he'd expected to find was that they had very high concentrations of food.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But to his surprise, that was only true for four of the hotspots.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The remaining hotspots were quite badly off in that regard.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But what he did find was that in all cases, the water at the surface of the ocean had relatively high temperatures.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Even when it was cool at greater depths, so, this seemed to be a factor in supporting a diverse range of these large predators.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, this wasn't enough on its own, because he also found that the water needed to have enough oxygen in it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So these two factors seem necessary to support the high metabolic rate of these large fish.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A couple of years later, in 2007, a researcher called Lisa Balance, who was working in California, also started looking for ocean hotspots.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But not for fish.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What she was interested in was marine mammals, things like seals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And she found three places in the oceans which were hotspots.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And what these had in common was that these hotspots were all located at boundaries between ocean currents, and this seems to be the sort of place that has lots of the plankton that some of these species feed on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So now people who want to protect the species that are endangered need to get as much information as possible.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, there's an international project called the Census of Marine Life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They've been surveying oceans all over the world, including the Arctic.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One thing they found there, which stunned other researchers, was that there were large numbers of species which live below the ice.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Sometimes under a layer up to 20 meters thick.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some of these species had never been seen before.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They've even found species of octopus living in these conditions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And other scientists working on the same project, but researching very different habitats on the ocean floor have found large numbers of species congregating around volcanoes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Attracted to them by the warmth and nutrients there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, biologists still don't know how serious the threat to their survival is for each individual species.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So a body called the Global Marine Species Assessment is now creating a list of endangered species on land, so they consider things like the size of the population, how many members of one species there are in a particular place.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And then they look at their distribution in geographical terms, although this is quite difficult when you're looking at fish, because they're so mobile, and then thirdly, they calculate the rate at which the decline of the species is happening.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So far, only 1,500 species have been assessed, but they want to increase this figure to 20,000 for each one they assess.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They use the data they collect on that species to produce a map showing its distribution.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Ultimately, they will be able to use these to figure out not only where most species are located.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But also where they are most threatened.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So finally, what can be done to retain the diversity of species in the world's oceans?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Firstly, we need to set up more reserves in our oceans, places where marine species are protected.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We have some, but not enough.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In addition, to preserve species such as leatherback turtles, which live out in the high seas, but have their nesting sites on the American coast.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We need to create corridors for migration, so they can get from one area to another safely.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As well as this, action needs to be taken to lower the levels of fishing quotas.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To prevent overfishing of endangered species. And finally, there's the problem of 'by-catch'.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This refers to the catching of unwanted fish by fishing boats.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They're returned to the sea, but they're often dead or dying.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If these commercial fishing boats used equipment which was more selective, so that only the fish wanted for consumption were caught.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This problem could be overcome.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So does anyone have any questions...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear part of a student presentation about the variety of different species that live in the world's oceans.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: I've been looking at ocean biodiversity.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's the diversity of species that live in the world's oceans.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: About 20 years ago, biologists developed the idea of what they called biodiversity hotspots.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These are the areas which have the greatest mixture of species, so one example is Madagascar.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These hotspots are significant.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Because they allow us to locate key areas for focusing efforts at conservation.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Biologists can identify hotspots on land fairly easily.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But until recently, very little was known about species distribution and diversity in the oceans, and no one even knew if hotspots existed there.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then a Canadian biologist called Boris Worm did some research in 2005 on data on ocean species that he got from the fishing industry.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Worm located five hotspots for large ocean predators like sharks, and looked at what they had in common.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The main thing he'd expected to find was that they had very high concentrations of food.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But to his surprise, that was only true for four of the hotspots.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The remaining hotspots were quite badly off in that regard.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But what he did find was that in all cases, the water at the surface of the ocean had relatively high temperatures.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Even when it was cool at greater depths, so, this seemed to be a factor in supporting a diverse range of these large predators.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, this wasn't enough on its own, because he also found that the water needed to have enough oxygen in it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So these two factors seem necessary to support the high metabolic rate of these large fish.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A couple of years later, in 2007, a researcher called Lisa Balance, who was working in California, also started looking for ocean hotspots.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But not for fish.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What she was interested in was marine mammals, things like seals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And she found three places in the oceans which were hotspots.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And what these had in common was that these hotspots were all located at boundaries between ocean currents, and this seems to be the sort of place that has lots of the plankton that some of these species feed on.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So now people who want to protect the species that are endangered need to get as much information as possible.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, there's an international project called the Census of Marine Life.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some of these species had never been seen before.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They've even found species of octopus living in these conditions.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And other scientists working on the same project, but researching very different habitats on the ocean floor have found large numbers of species congregating around volcanoes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Attracted to them by the warmth and nutrients there.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, biologists still don't know how serious the threat to their survival is for each individual species.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So a body called the Global Marine Species Assessment is now creating a list of endangered species on land, so they consider things like the size of the population, how many members of one species there are in a particular place.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And then they look at their distribution in geographical terms, although this is quite difficult when you're looking at fish, because they're so mobile, and then thirdly, they calculate the rate at which the decline of the species is happening.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Ultimately, they will be able to use these to figure out not only where most species are located.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But also where they are most threatened.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So finally, what can be done to retain the diversity of species in the world's oceans?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We have some, but not enough.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In addition, to preserve species such as leatherback turtles, which live out in the high seas, but have their nesting sites on the American coast.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We need to create corridors for migration, so they can get from one area to another safely.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As well as this, action needs to be taken to lower the levels of fishing quotas.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To prevent overfishing of endangered species. And finally, there's the problem of 'by-catch'.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This refers to the catching of unwanted fish by fishing boats.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They're returned to the sea, but they're often dead or dying.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This problem could be overcome.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER
